competition.name: SecureByte Society
competition.welcome_message: Welcome to SecureByte Society CTF — find the flags, sharpen your skills, and climb the leaderboard.

challenges.title: Decode the Message
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: A mysterious file has been found with scrambled text. It looks like someone tried to hide data using a common encoding scheme. Decode it layer by layer to reveal the flag.
steps: Open challenge1.txt — notice the base64-looking text
Run:  base64 -d challenge1.txt
The output is still base64! Decode it a second time.
Or use CyberChef → From Base64 (apply twice)
Python:  import base64; print(base64.b64decode(base64.b64decode(open('challenge1.txt').read())))
hints: The = signs at the end are Base64 padding — it has been encoded more than once.
Try decoding twice:  base64 -d challenge1.txt | base64 -d
The flag format is: flag{...}

challenges.title: Caesar's Secret
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: Julius Caesar used a simple cipher to protect his messages — shift every letter by a fixed number. A message has been intercepted. Shift it back to read the secret.
steps: Download and open caesar.txt
Notice the text looks like English but every letter is shifted
Try CyberChef → ROT13 → adjust the Amount to 3
Or Python: ''.join(chr((ord(c)-ord('a')-3)%26+ord('a')) if c.islower() else c for c in text)
Caesar cipher uses a shift of 3 — A becomes D, B becomes E, etc.
hints: Julius Caesar always used a shift of 3 — A becomes D, B becomes E, etc.
In CyberChef use 'ROT13' with Amount=3, or try 'Caesar Box Cipher'.
The flag is inside the decoded message — look for flag{...}

challenges.title: ROT13 Riddle
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: ROT13 is a special Caesar cipher that rotates letters by 13 positions. It is its own inverse — applying it twice gives you back the original. Decode this intercepted message.
steps: Download rot13.txt and read its contents
Apply ROT13: in Linux terminal run:  cat rot13.txt | tr 'A-Za-z' 'N-ZA-Mn-za-m'
Or CyberChef → ROT13 (default settings)
Or Python:  import codecs; codecs.decode(open('rot13.txt').read(), 'rot_13')
hints: ROT13 means rotate letters by 13 — A becomes N, B becomes O, etc.
Linux one-liner:  cat rot13.txt | tr 'A-Za-z' 'N-ZA-Mn-za-m'
The decoded message contains the flag — look for flag{...}

challenges.title: Hex Decoder
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: Data can be represented in hexadecimal — base 16 using digits 0-9 and letters A-F. A secret has been encoded as a stream of hex bytes. Convert it back to text to find the flag.
steps: Open hexfile.txt — you will see a long string of 0-9 and a-f characters
Each pair of hex characters is one ASCII character
Python:  bytes.fromhex(open('hexfile.txt').read().strip()).decode()
Or CyberChef → From Hex
Or terminal:  echo '666c61677b...' | xxd -r -p
hints: Hexadecimal is base 16 — each byte is two hex digits: 66=f, 6c=l, 61=a, 67=g
Try: python3 -c "print(bytes.fromhex(open('hexfile.txt').read().strip()).decode())"
The first 8 hex characters 666c6167 spell out 'flag'

challenges.title: XOR Breaker
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: XOR encryption XORs each byte of the plaintext with a key. Luckily, XOR is its own inverse — XOR the ciphertext with the same key to get back the plaintext. The key is given. Decrypt it.
steps: Open xor_cipher.txt — note the key (0x42) and the hex ciphertext
XOR every byte of the ciphertext with 0x42
Python: cipher=bytes.fromhex('PASTE_HEX'); print(bytes([b^0x42 for b in cipher]).decode())
Or CyberChef → From Hex → XOR (key: 42, scheme: Hex)
hints: XOR is its own inverse: if A XOR B = C, then C XOR B = A
Copy the hex string from the file and XOR each byte with 0x42 (decimal 66)
Python one-liner: bytes([b^0x42 for b in bytes.fromhex('HEX_HERE')]).decode()

challenges.title: Fake Image
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: Someone sent a file called image.jpg — but something is very wrong. The file is lying about what it really is. Find out what is hiding inside.
steps: Run:  file image.jpg — notice it says 'Zip archive data', not JPEG!
Run:  binwalk image.jpg — spot the embedded ZIP signature
Rename:  mv image.jpg image.zip
Extract:  unzip image.zip — read flag.txt inside
hints: A file extension is just a label — the real type is stored in the first bytes (magic bytes).
binwalk scans for known file headers. Look for a ZIP offset at position 0x00.
ZIP magic bytes are: PK.. (50 4B 03 04)

challenges.title: Metadata Hunt
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: A photo was shared online. The person thought they were anonymous — but photos carry invisible metadata that can expose secrets. Hunt down the hidden flag.
steps: Run:  exiftool photo.jpg — read all metadata fields carefully
Look for the Author and Flag fields in the output
Or:  strings photo.jpg | grep flag
Or:  cat photo.jpg — this file is plain text disguised as a JPG!
hints: EXIF metadata is embedded in image files and can store camera model, GPS, author, comments...
Try:  strings photo.jpg
This file is actually plain text — try opening it in a text editor directly!

challenges.title: Whitespace Stego
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: Steganography is hiding information inside innocent-looking files. This text file looks completely normal — but a secret is hidden in the invisible whitespace characters. Can you extract it?
steps: Open whitespace.txt — it looks like a normal text file
Run:  cat -A whitespace.txt — look for ^ (spaces) and I (tabs) at the end
The flag is encoded in trailing whitespace: space=0, tab=1 (binary)

Python snippet: line=[l for l in open('whitespace.txt').readlines() if set(l.strip())<=set(' \t')][0]; bits=''.join('1' if c=='\t' else '0' for c in line); print(''.join(chr(int(bits[i:i+8],2)) for i in range(0,len(bits),8)))
hints: The hidden data is entirely invisible — it uses only spaces and tabs which look identical in a text editor.
Run: cat -A whitespace.txt and look for a line made entirely of ^ (space) and I (tab) characters.
Decode rule: space=0, tab=1, groups of 8 bits = one ASCII character

challenges.title: Hidden Strings
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: A mysterious binary file was found on a compromised server. Somewhere inside its bytes is a hidden message. Use the right tools to extract it.
steps: Run:  strings mystery.bin — extract all printable text sequences
Run:  strings mystery.bin | grep flag — filter for the flag
Or:  xxd mystery.bin | less — look through the hex dump
Or:  cat mystery.bin | tr -cd '[:print:]' | grep -o 'flag{[^}]*}'
hints: The 'strings' command extracts sequences of 4+ printable ASCII characters from any file.
Try:  strings -n 4 mystery.bin | grep flag
The flag is embedded directly in the binary between non-printable bytes.

challenges.title: CrackMe
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: A program has been found on a suspicious server. It checks for a password before revealing a reward. You cannot run it — but you do not need to. The password and the flag are embedded inside the binary itself.
steps: Run:  strings crackme.bin — look through all embedded text
Find the usage message, error message, and success message
The flag is printed when the correct password is entered
Also look for the password itself — it is stored as a plain string
Run:  strings crackme.bin | grep -E 'flag|pass|correct|reward'
hints: Real-world crackme challenges require reverse engineering — but this one stores everything in plaintext.
Run: strings crackme.bin and read every line carefully.
Look for a string that looks like a password near the success message.

challenges.title: Client-Side Secrets
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: Open zzz.html in your browser. The developer made a classic mistake — they stored a secret inside the JavaScript code. Find it using your browser's DevTools.
steps: Download and open zzz.html in your browser
Press Ctrl+Shift+I (or Cmd+Opt+I on Mac) to open DevTools
Go to the Sources tab and open zzz.html
Search for 'FLAG{' using Ctrl+F inside the source viewer
OR: Go to the Console tab, enter any login, click Login — watch for debug output
hints: Look for a variable named FLAG or const FLAG in the <script> section.
The developer left DEBUG_MODE = true — secrets get logged to the browser console.
Open Console tab, type anything in the login form, click Login. Read what appears.

challenges.title: Encoded Login
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: Open zzzzchallenge.html — there is an admin login page. The credentials are hidden using Base64 encoding. Decode them, log in, and capture the flag.
steps: Download and open zzzzchallenge.html in your browser
Open DevTools → Sources — find const encodedUser and const encodedPass
Open the Console tab and run:  atob('YWRtaW4=')
Run:  atob('cGFzc3dvcmQxMjM=')  to get the password
Log in with the decoded credentials to reveal the flag
hints: atob() is a browser built-in function that decodes Base64 strings.
Open the Console tab and type:  atob('YWRtaW4=')  then press Enter.
Username: admin  |  Password: password123 — now log in to get the flag!

challenges.title: SQL Injection
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: A staff login portal has been found. The developer forgot to sanitize the username input — which means you can inject SQL commands directly into the login query. Bypass the login without knowing the password.
steps: Download and open login_sqli.html in your browser
The SQL query is:  SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='INPUT' AND password='INPUT'
Try entering this as the username:  ' OR '1'='1
This makes the query always true, bypassing the password check
Or try:  admin'--  which comments out the password check entirely
hints: SQL injection uses special characters to break out of the intended query structure.
The single quote ' ends the username string. Everything after becomes SQL code.
Try the username:  ' OR '1'='1  — this makes the WHERE clause always true.

challenges.title: Cookie Monster
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: A VIP members area controls access using a browser cookie. Guest users cannot see the flag. But cookies are just text stored in your browser — and you can edit them. Can you upgrade your own access?
steps: Download and open cookie_auth.html in your browser
Click 'Check My Access' — you will see you are set as a guest
Open DevTools → Application tab → Cookies (left sidebar)
Find the cookie named 'role' and double-click its value
Change the value from 'guest' to 'vip' and press Enter, then refresh
hints: Browser cookies are visible and editable in DevTools → Application → Cookies.
Find the 'role' cookie and change its value to something with higher privileges.
Try changing role to: vip

challenges.title: Packet Secrets
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: A network capture log was found on a suspicious machine. Somewhere in the captured frames is a secret token. Decode it to find the flag.
steps: Open capture.log and read through the captured network frames
Look at Frame 5 — notice the Authorization: Bearer token
A JWT has three base64-encoded parts separated by dots
Decode the second part (the payload) using base64
Python:  import base64; base64.b64decode('SECOND_PART' + '==').decode()
hints: A JWT token has the format: header.payload.signature — decode the middle part.
The payload is base64 encoded. Add == padding if needed before decoding.
Python: import base64, json; print(json.loads(base64.b64decode('PAYLOAD==')))

challenges.title: Morse Mayhem
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: A distress signal was intercepted from a compromised radio station. The message is in Morse code — dots and dashes that map to letters and numbers. Decode the transmission to find the flag.
steps: Open morse.txt and read the Morse code pattern
Each letter is separated by a space, each word by ' / '
Use CyberChef → From Morse Code
Or visit: https://morsecode.world/international/translator.html
Or dcode.fr → Morse Code — paste the message and decode
hints: Use an online Morse decoder — CyberChef has a built-in 'From Morse Code' operation.
The flag starts with FLAG{ — once decoded it will be clear!
. = dot (short), - = dash (long). Letters are separated by spaces, words by /

challenges.title: Vigenere Vault
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: The Vigenere cipher uses a keyword to shift letters by different amounts, making it much harder to crack than Caesar. A message has been intercepted. The key length is 5 letters and is a common cybersecurity word.
steps: Download vigenere.txt and read the ciphertext
The cipher uses a repeating keyword to shift each letter
Method 1 — CyberChef: paste text → add 'Vigenere Decode' → try key: CYBER
Method 2 — dcode.fr/vigenere-cipher → paste ciphertext → try key CYBER
Method 3 — Python: write a decrypt function with key='CYBER'
The decrypted message contains the flag inside flag{...}
hints: The key is 5 letters long — the file tells you this.
The key is a well-known cybersecurity word. Think about what field we are in.
Key: CYBER — try it in CyberChef → Vigenere Decode

challenges.title: Binary Basics
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: Computers store everything as binary — ones and zeros. Each group of 8 bits is one byte, which maps to one ASCII character. Convert the binary stream back to text to reveal the flag.
steps: Download binary.txt — you will see groups of 8 digits (0s and 1s)
Each 8-digit group is one ASCII character in binary
Method 1 — CyberChef: paste the binary → add 'From Binary' recipe
Method 2 — Python: bits='01100110 01101100 ...'; print(''.join(chr(int(b,2)) for b in bits.split()))
Method 3 — visit rapidtables.com/convert/number/binary-to-ascii.html
The converted text is the flag
hints: In CyberChef just use 'From Binary' — it handles spaces automatically
Python: ''.join(chr(int(b,2)) for b in open('binary.txt').read().split('\n')[2].split())

challenges.title: JWT Forgery
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: You have been issued a guest JWT token. The server uses alg:none — meaning it does NOT verify the signature. Forge a new JWT with role=admin in the payload to get the flag.
steps: Download jwt_challenge.txt — read your guest token
A JWT is: base64url(header).base64url(payload).signature
Since alg is 'none', the signature is empty — just a trailing dot
Step 1: decode the payload (middle part) to understand its structure
Step 2: create a new payload with role changed from 'user' to 'admin'
Step 3: base64url-encode your new payload (no padding =)
Step 4: build the new token: original_header.new_payload.
Python: import base64,json; p=json.dumps({'user':'admin','role':'admin','flag':'?'}); token=header+'.'+base64.b64encode(p.encode()).rstrip(b'=').decode()+'.'
The admin token from the file already contains the flag in the payload — just decode it!
hints: The admin JWT token is already in jwt_challenge.txt — decode its payload!
Take the second part (between the two dots), add == and base64 decode it
Python: import base64; base64.b64decode('SECOND_PART==').decode()

challenges.title: Hash Cracker
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: A database of password hashes has been leaked. The admin account uses an MD5 hash. MD5 is a one-way function but common passwords can be cracked using rainbow tables or wordlists. Crack it.
steps: Download hashes.txt — find the admin MD5 hash
Method 1 (easiest): visit crackstation.net — paste the MD5 hash and click Crack
Method 2 — Python brute force with common passwords:
import hashlib
wordlist=['password','123456','admin','letmein','qwerty','dragon','master','hello','welcome','sunshine']
target='PASTE_ADMIN_HASH'
for p in wordlist:
if hashlib.md5(p.encode()).hexdigest()==target: print('Password found:',p)
The flag is: flag{the_cracked_password} where the_cracked_password is what you found
hints: MD5 hashes of common passwords are publicly available in rainbow tables.
Try crackstation.net — just paste the hash, it has billions of pre-cracked hashes.
The password is a common English word — it is in the top 100 most common passwords.

challenges.title: Path Traversal
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: A file viewer web app reads files from a fixed directory. But it does not validate the filename — meaning you can use ../ to escape the intended directory and read any file on the server, including /etc/passwd.
steps: Download and open traversal.html in your browser
The app reads files from /var/www/files/ — try typing report.txt
Now try: ../secret.txt — notice it goes up one directory!
Try: ../../etc/passwd — this goes up two levels and reads the system password file
Or try: /etc/passwd directly
Read the output carefully — the flag is hidden in one of the user entries
hints: ../ means 'go up one directory'. ../../ goes up two levels.
Try ../../etc/passwd in the filename field
The flag is in the user entry for flag_user in the passwd output

challenges.title: LSB Stego
challenges.author: SBS Team
challenges.description: LSB (Least Significant Bit) steganography hides data by replacing the last bit of each pixel's colour value. The change is invisible to the human eye but extractable with the right tools. A report file shows you the extraction results.
steps: Download lsb_report.txt
Read the tool output carefully — it shows what zsteg found
Look at the [b1,r,lsb,xy] line — this is the red channel LSB extraction
The flag is directly visible in the report output
In a real LSB challenge you would run: zsteg -a image.png
Or use Python: from PIL import Image; img=Image.open('img.png'); pixels=list(img.getdata()); bits=''.join(str(p[0]&1) for p in pixels)
hints: Read the report file carefully — the flag is printed in plain text in the output
Look at the [b1,r,lsb,xy] entry in the zsteg output
In real LSB stego, the flag is reconstructed from the least significant bits of each pixel
